title: Match-Case (Pattern Matching)
description: Test your understanding of Python's match-case statement, pattern matching, wildcard default cases, and the pipe operator.
subject: Programming

questionText: What Python version introduced the match-case statement?
subject: Programming

content.explanation: The match-case statement was added in Python 3.10. It's a newer feature for pattern matching.

questionText: What does the underscore _ mean in a match-case block?
subject: Programming

options.text: It's the default case -- matches anything not caught above
It skips the match block entirely

content.explanation: The _ (underscore) is the wildcard pattern. It acts like a catch-all default, running when none of the other cases match.

questionText:
What is the output of this code?
command = "go"
match command:
    case "stop":
        print("Stopping")
    case "go":
        print("Moving")
    case _:
        print("Unknown")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Python checks each case top-to-bottom. command is 'go', which matches the second case, so it prints 'Moving'.

questionText: What does the | symbol do inside a case statement?
subject: Programming

options.text: Matches multiple values in one case (like OR)

content.explanation: The | (pipe) lets you match several values with one case. For example, case 'a' | 'b' matches if the value is 'a' OR 'b'.

questionText:
What is the output?
day = "Saturday"
match day:
    case "Saturday" | "Sunday":
        print("Weekend!")
    case _:
        print("Weekday")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: The | operator lets both 'Saturday' and 'Sunday' match the same case. Since day is 'Saturday', it matches and prints 'Weekend!'.

questionText: What happens if NO case matches and there's no case _ (wildcard)?
subject: Programming

options.text: Nothing happens -- the program continues past the match block
It goes back to the first case

content.explanation: If nothing matches and there's no wildcard _, Python simply skips the entire match block and moves on. No error occurs.

questionText: When should you use match-case instead of if-elif-else?
subject: Programming

options.text: When checking a variable against specific exact values
When you need complex boolean conditions

content.explanation: match-case is ideal for matching exact values (like menu choices or commands). For ranges or complex conditions, if-elif-else is better.

questionText:
What is the output?
grade = "B"
match grade:
    case "A":
        print("Excellent")
    case "B":
        print("Good")
    case "C":
        print("Average")
    case "B":
        print("Nice")
    case _:
        print("Try again")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Only the FIRST matching case runs. Even though 'B' appears twice, the first case 'B' matches first and prints 'Good'. Python then exits the match block.

questionText:
What does this code output?
choice = 3
match choice:
    case 1:
        print("One")
    case 2:
        print("Two")
    case 3:
        print("Three")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: match-case works with numbers too! choice is 3, which matches case 3, so it prints 'Three'.

questionText:
What is the output?
color = "green"
match color:
    case "red":
        print("Stop")
    case "yellow":
        print("Caution")
    case "green":
        print("Go")
    case _:
        print("Invalid")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: color is 'green', which matches the third case. Python prints 'Go' -- just like following a traffic light!

questionText:
Will this match-case work correctly?
temp = "hot"
match temp:
    case "Hot":
        print("Stay cool!")
    case _:
        print("Unknown")
subject: Programming

options.text: It prints 'Unknown' because 'hot' and 'Hot' are different

content.explanation: Python is case-sensitive! 'hot' does NOT match 'Hot'. Since no case matches, the wildcard _ catches it and prints 'Unknown'.

questionText:
What is the output?
op = "+"
a, b = 10, 3
match op:
    case "+":
        print(a + b)
    case "-":
        print(a - b)
    case "*":
        print(a * b)
    case _:
        print("Unknown operator")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: op is '+', which matches the first case. It prints a + b = 10 + 3 = 13. This is a simple calculator using match-case!

questionText:
What is the output?
season = "March"
match season:
    case "Dec" | "Jan" | "Feb":
        print("Winter")
    case "Mar" | "Apr" | "May":
        print("Spring")
    case _:
        print("Other")
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Tricky! The variable is 'March' but the case checks for 'Mar'. They're not the same string, so no case matches and _ catches it.

questionText: In which situation is if-elif-else better than match-case?
subject: Programming

options.text: Checking if a score is above 90, above 80, above 70, etc.
Checking if a direction is 'north', 'south', 'east', or 'west'
Checking if a menu choice is 1, 2, 3, or 4
Checking if a color is 'red', 'blue', or 'green'

content.explanation: Ranges (score > 90) need comparison operators, which match-case doesn't support well. if-elif-else handles ranges and complex conditions better.

questionText: Which of these are true about Python's match-case?
subject: Programming

options.text: Only the first matching case runs
The _ wildcard catches everything not matched above
You can use | to match multiple values in one case
All matching cases run one after another

content.explanation: match-case runs only the first match (a), _ is the catch-all (b), and | combines values (c). It was added in Python 3.10 (not 2.0), and cases don't fall through.

questionText: In a match-case block, it's a good practice to always include a case _ as the last case.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Yes! Including case _ ensures unexpected values are handled instead of silently ignored. It's like having an 'else' in if-elif.

questionText: match-case in Python works like a switch statement in other languages and falls through to the next case automatically.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Unlike switch in C/Java, Python's match-case does NOT fall through. Once a case matches, only that case runs and the match block ends.

questionText: To match multiple values in one case, you use the ___ symbol between values.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: The | (pipe) symbol acts like 'or'. case 'a' | 'b' matches if the value is 'a' OR 'b'.

questionText: The default catch-all pattern in match-case uses the symbol ___.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: The underscore _ is the wildcard pattern. It matches anything that wasn't caught by the cases above it.

questionText: Match each match-case concept to its description.
subject: Programming

rightItems.text: Defines a pattern to check against
Combines multiple values in one case

content.explanation: match starts the block, case defines each pattern, _ catches everything else, and | lets you OR patterns together.

questionText: Match each scenario to the best approach.
subject: Programming

leftItems.text: Check if age > 18 and has ID

content.explanation: match-case is great for exact values (menus, commands). if-elif handles ranges, comparisons, and complex conditions better.

questionText: Put these parts of a match-case block in the correct order.
subject: Programming

content.explanation: Start with match and the variable, then list specific cases with their code, and end with the _ wildcard as the default.
